blocks.body: Can / Could helps you talk about ability, possibility, basic permission, and polite requests.

{"level":"A1-A2","intro":"In this topic you will see how can and could work for ability, possibility, permission, and polite requests.","rules":[{"label":"ABILITY NOW","color":"emerald","text":"Can talks about present or general ability.","example":"I can swim. / She can drive."},{"label":"ABILITY THEN","color":"blue","text":"Could often shows general ability in the past or a softer request.","example":"When I was ten, I could swim. / Could you help me?"},{"label":"REQUESTS / POSSIBILITY","color":"amber","text":"Can and could also appear in permission, requests, and some meanings of possibility.","example":"Can I sit here? / It could rain later."}]}

{"title":"1. Key forms","intro":"After can and could, we use the base verb without to.","items":[{"label":"Present / general","title":"can + V","subtitle":"can swim / can't swim / Can you swim?"},{"label":"Past / polite","title":"could + V","subtitle":"could swim / couldn't swim / Could you help?"},{"label":"One successful action","title":"was able to / managed to","subtitle":"Yesterday I was able to solve it."},{"label":"Longer forms","title":"be able to","subtitle":"will be able to / want to be able to"}]}

{"title":"2. Main uses","sections":[{"label":"Ability","color":"emerald","description":"Use can for present or general ability, and could for general ability in the past.","examples":[{"en":"I can cook, but I can't bake well.","ua":"I can cook, but I can't bake well."},{"en":"My grandfather could speak three languages.","ua":"My grandfather could speak three languages."},{"en":"She will be able to join us next week.","ua":"She will be able to join us next week."}],"note":"For one specific successful action in the past, we often use was able to or managed to."},{"label":"Permission and requests","color":"blue","description":"Can works for simple permission or direct requests. Could makes the request softer and more polite.","examples":[{"en":"Can I open the window?","ua":"Can I open the window?"},{"en":"Can you call me after class?","ua":"Can you call me after class?"},{"en":"Could you send me the file again?","ua":"Could you send me the file again?"}]},{"label":"Possibility","color":"amber","description":"Can often shows general possibility, while could shows a more specific possibility or a less certain result.","examples":[{"en":"This road can be dangerous in winter.","ua":"This road can be dangerous in winter."},{"en":"It could rain this evening.","ua":"It could rain this evening."},{"en":"We could be late if we leave now.","ua":"We could be late if we leave now."}],"note":"For a positive specific possibility, speakers more often use could, may, or might than can."}]}

{"title":"3. Common mistakes","items":[{"label":"Mistake 1","color":"rose","title":"Adding -s to can.","wrong":"She cans swim well.","right":"✅ She can swim well.","hint":"Modal verbs do not change for person."},{"label":"Mistake 2","color":"amber","title":"Using to after a modal.","wrong":"I can to help you.","right":"✅ I can help you.","hint":"After can / could, use the bare infinitive: help, not to help."},{"label":"Mistake 3","color":"sky","title":"Using could for one specific successful past action.","wrong":"Yesterday I could fix the printer at last.","right":"✅ Yesterday I was able to fix the printer at last.","hint":"Could more often shows general past ability, not one completed success."}]}

{"title":"4. Quick rule","items":["Can = present or general ability, simple permission, direct request.","Could = general past ability, a more polite request, or a specific possibility.","After can / could, use the base verb without to.","For one successful past action, was able to or managed to is often better.","Could you...? sounds softer than Can you...?."]}
